stem: The nurse is caring for a 19-year-old male who sustained blunt chest trauma in a motor vehicle collision. The client suddenly develops severe respiratory distress and hypotension. Based on the electronic health record, complete the bowtie by selecting the most likely condition, two priority nursing actions, and two parameters to monitor.

tabs.title: SBAR
tabs.content: Situation: Nurse Alex in the ED. My 19-year-old male patient from an MVC is acutely decompensating with severe respiratory distress and hypotension. Background: He arrived two hours ago with blunt chest trauma and has rapidly worsened in the last 15 minutes. Assessment: At 14:00, his vitals are BP 78/40, HR 148, RR 36, SpO2 76%. He has significant JVD, absent right-sided breath sounds, and tracheal deviation to the left. Recommendation: I suspect a tension pneumothorax. I need an immediate provider for needle decompression and am applying 100% oxygen.

tabs.title: Vital Signs
tabs.content:
Time: 14:00
Blood Pressure: 78/40 mmHg
Heart Rate: 148 bpm
Respiratory Rate: 36 breaths/min
SpO2: 76% on room air

tabs.title: Lab Results
tabs.content: No lab results are currently available. Arterial blood gas (ABG) has been ordered.

tabs.title: Assessment Findings
tabs.content:
Assessment ItemFinding
Breath SoundsAbsent on right side; Hyper-resonant to percussion
Tracheal PositionDeviated to the left
Venous StatusJugular venous distention (JVD) present
Chest X-ray (Portable)Ordered, results pending

tabs.title: MAR
tabs.content: No medications have been administered at this time.

actionOptions.text: Administer IV Heparin bolus as prescribed
Assess for Homan's sign in lower extremities

parameterOptions.text: Presence of a cardiac friction rub

hoverRationales.c1: This client's unilateral absent breath sounds, hyper-resonance, and tracheal deviation are not characteristic of a pulmonary embolism. These signs point to a pathology involving air in the pleural space, not a vascular obstruction.
hoverRationales.c2: This condition occurs when air enters the pleural space and cannot escape, causing lung collapse, mediastinal shift, and obstructive shock. The client's presentation of hypotension, JVD, unilateral absent breath sounds, and tracheal deviation are classic signs.
hoverRationales.c3: While cardiac tamponade can cause hypotension and JVD (Beck's triad), it would present with muffled heart sounds and equal breath sounds bilaterally. The client's unilateral respiratory findings make this diagnosis unlikely.
hoverRationales.a1: This is a life-saving intervention that rapidly releases trapped air from the pleural space. It converts the tension pneumothorax to a simple pneumothorax, restoring venous return and improving cardiopulmonary function.
hoverRationales.a2: The client is severely hypoxic with an SpO2 of 76%. Maximizing oxygen delivery is a critical priority to support tissue perfusion while definitive treatment is being prepared.
hoverRationales.a3: Heparin is an anticoagulant used for pulmonary embolism, not a tension pneumothorax. Administering it would be inappropriate and could worsen potential bleeding from the chest trauma.
hoverRationales.a4: Homan's sign is an unreliable assessment for deep vein thrombosis, a potential cause of pulmonary embolism. This assessment is irrelevant to the client's acute respiratory collapse from a tension pneumothorax.
hoverRationales.p1: Monitoring the trachea is crucial as its return to a midline position is a key indicator of successful decompression. Re-deviation would signal a recurrence of the tension.
hoverRationales.p2: The return of breath sounds on the affected side confirms lung re-expansion after decompression. Continued bilateral assessment is essential to monitor the effectiveness of the intervention.
hoverRationales.p3: This parameter is used to monitor for deep vein thrombosis or edema over time. It is not relevant for assessing the immediate, life-threatening condition of a tension pneumothorax.
hoverRationales.p4: A pericardial friction rub is a sign of pericarditis, which is not indicated by the client's presentation. The priority is to monitor respiratory and hemodynamic status related to the pneumothorax.
